purpose: Canonical node-type proposal for system-design interview diagrams. The goal is to make diagram styling and captions consistent without turning every implementation detail into a first-class type.
principles: Types should describe deployable or externally visible architecture roles candidates commonly draw in interviews.
Types should be few enough to scan quickly during an interview walkthrough.
Statefulness is a trait of compute nodes, not a separate primary type.
Policy is usually behavior inside a gateway, service, worker, or data store, not a primary node type.
Actor is reserved for human or organizational roles; client is used for software surfaces such as browsers, mobile apps, SDKs, portals, and upstream caller applications.
Explicit node metadata is the source of truth. Rendering code should not infer node types from ids or labels.
A component that writes state is not automatically stateful. It is stateful only when local runtime state affects correctness, routing, ownership, or failover.

nodes.label: Display label exactly as it appears in Mermaid
nodes.description: Short role description for this node in this interview.

links.label: HTTPS
links.description: Client sends requests to the API boundary.

links.label: read/write
links.description: API service reads and writes authoritative state.

actor.label: Actor
actor.definition: A human or organizational role that initiates, receives, approves, or operates interactions with the system.
useWhen: The node is outside the designed backend boundary.
The node represents a person, user role, business role, admin, operator, merchant, customer, creator, viewer, driver, or other human/organization actor.
notFor: Browsers, mobile apps, SDKs, portals, dashboards, devices, or producer/consumer applications; use client.
Internal services that happen to call another service.
Third-party managed systems; use external.

client.label: Client
client.definition: A software surface outside the designed backend boundary that initiates requests, receives responses, or integrates with the system as a caller.
useWhen: The node is a browser, mobile app, desktop app, SDK, CLI, portal, dashboard, admin tool, device application, producer application, consumer application, or upstream caller service.
The design needs to distinguish the user or organization from the software they use to reach the backend.
notFor: Human roles; use actor.
Backend API gateways, BFFs, ingress routers, or load balancers operated by the system; use gateway.
Third-party managed systems; use external.

edge.label: Edge
edge.definition: A geographically or network-edge component that terminates or accelerates traffic before it reaches the core system.
useWhen: The node is a CDN, edge cache, DNS edge, edge POP, or static asset delivery layer.
The node's main role is edge placement or global delivery.
notFor: API gateways, ingress routers, load balancers, admission gates, or WAFs; use gateway.

gateway.label: Gateway
gateway.definition: An ingress, routing, admission, or traffic-shaping component at a service boundary.
useWhen: The node routes or gates requests before business logic.
The node provides load balancing, API gateway behavior, ingress, WAF, admission control, rate limiting, or shard/region routing.
notFor: A domain API service that owns business logic; use service.
A cache/CDN edge; use edge.

service.label: Service
service.definition: A synchronous application component that handles requests and owns business logic.
useWhen: The node is a backend service, API implementation, application server, model-serving wrapper, or domain component.
No more specific compute type such as orchestrator, worker, or gateway applies.
notFor: A workflow coordinator; use orchestrator.
A background processor; use worker.
A traffic boundary component; use gateway.

orchestrator.label: Orchestrator
orchestrator.definition: A component whose main role is coordinating multi-step workflows, sagas, fan-out/fan-in, or cross-service state transitions.
useWhen: The node sequences several operations and owns workflow state or compensation logic.
The interview needs to discuss transaction boundaries, retries, compensation, or state-machine transitions.
notFor: A simple stateless service that calls one downstream dependency.
A shard router or load balancer; use gateway.

worker.label: Worker
worker.definition: An asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.
useWhen: The node consumes from a queue or stream.
The node performs batch jobs, scheduled jobs, media processing, crawling, aggregation, compaction, cleanup, training, or reconciliation.
notFor: Synchronous user-facing services; use service.
ML inference components; use model when the model itself is the architectural concern.

queue.label: Queue
queue.definition: A work queue or broker abstraction used to buffer discrete tasks/messages for consumers.
useWhen: The main semantics are enqueue, dequeue, retry, visibility timeout, dead-lettering, or worker fan-out.
The queue exists to absorb bursts or decouple producers from consumers.
notFor: Append-only event streams, CDC logs, or Kafka-like ordered logs; use stream.
Persistent commit logs used as storage internals; use database or object-storage depending on the context.

stream.label: Stream
stream.definition: An append-only event stream, durable log, event bus, or pub/sub fabric where ordering, replay, and offsets matter.
useWhen: The design discusses replay, partitions, offsets, consumer groups, event sourcing, CDC, or log retention.
Multiple consumers independently read the same event history.
notFor: Simple task queues where messages disappear after processing; use queue.

cache.label: Cache
cache.definition: A fast, usually derived or disposable state layer used to reduce latency or load on authoritative systems.
useWhen: The node can be rebuilt or repopulated from an authoritative source.
The design discusses TTLs, cache invalidation, hot keys, read-through/write-through behavior, or stale reads.
notFor: Authoritative state even when implemented in Redis; use database and add traits if needed.

database.label: Database
database.definition: An authoritative operational state store for structured records, key-value state, metadata, ledgers, locks, or transactional state.
useWhen: The node is the source of truth for correctness.
The design discusses transactions, consistency, constraints, indexes, replication, sharding, or durability.
notFor: Blob/file/media storage; use object-storage.
Search/vector/geo/prefix lookup structures; use index.
Disposable acceleration layers; use cache.

object-storage.label: Object Storage
object-storage.definition: Blob, media, file, archive, or origin storage optimized for large immutable or semi-immutable objects.
useWhen: The node stores videos, images, documents, pages, archives, blobs, files, or CDN origins.
The design discusses object keys, lifecycle policies, multipart upload, versioning, or large binary payloads.
notFor: Operational metadata or transactional state; use database.

index.label: Index
index.definition: A derived lookup, search, ranking, vector, prefix, geo, or inverted index used to retrieve candidates efficiently.
useWhen: The node is optimized for lookup/search rather than authoritative writes.
The design discusses rebuilds, index versions, ranking candidates, vector search, prefix search, or geo lookup.
notFor: The primary source of truth; use database.
A generic database secondary index hidden inside a DB node.

model.label: Model
model.definition: An ML/AI inference, ranking, embedding, classification, or reranking component where the model behavior is central to the design.
useWhen: The node represents a generative model, embedding model, ranker, reranker, classifier, recommendation model, or inference endpoint.
The interview needs to discuss latency, quality, model versions, fallback models, or evaluation.
notFor: Training jobs or offline batch pipelines; use worker unless the model artifact itself is the point.
A generic service that happens to call a model; use service.

observability.label: Observability
observability.definition: Telemetry, monitoring, alerting, logging, tracing, audit, dashboard, or operational feedback components.
useWhen: The node exists to understand system behavior or trigger operational response.
The design discusses SLOs, alerts, metrics, logs, traces, audit trails, or dashboards.
notFor: User-facing analytics products; use service, database, or index based on role.

external.label: External
external.definition: A third-party or separately managed system outside the design's operational ownership boundary.
useWhen: The node is an identity provider, payment processor, notification provider, external API, model provider, or web host.
The interview should reason about dependency failure, retries, quotas, idempotency, or vendor SLAs.
notFor: End users or organizations; use actor.
Client applications or upstream caller software; use client.

stateless.label: Stateless
stateless.definition: The component can be horizontally scaled or replaced because request correctness does not depend on local runtime state.
examples: API service reading all durable state from a database
Worker that can retry jobs from a queue

stateful.label: Stateful
stateful.definition: The component has local runtime state that affects correctness, routing, ownership, or failover.

sessionful.label: Sessionful
sessionful.definition: The component owns long-lived client sessions or connections even if durable state is elsewhere.

partitioned.label: Partitioned
partitioned.definition: The component is split by key, tenant, region, shard, topic partition, or event.

replicated.label: Replicated
replicated.definition: Multiple copies exist for availability, read scale, failover, or durability.

leader.label: Leader
leader.definition: One replica or node owns decisions for a partition, shard, cluster, or workflow at a time.

hot-path.label: Hot Path
hot-path.definition: The component is on the latency-sensitive request path and should be treated carefully in interview tradeoffs.

authoritative.label: Authoritative
authoritative.definition: The component is the source of truth for a correctness-critical invariant.
examples: External provider for accepted transaction state

derived.label: Derived
derived.definition: The component can be rebuilt from another source of truth.

policy.reason: Policy is usually a behavior or rule set, not a deployable architecture role. Model the component that enforces or stores it.
mapTo: gateway for WAF, rate-limit gateway, admission control
service for auth, fraud, or policy-decision services
database for rule stores or policy stores

api.reason: API is usually an interface, not an architecture role. It caused confusion between API gateway and domain API service.
mapTo: gateway for API Gateway or ingress
service for domain APIs that own business logic

app.reason: App is a delivery surface, not a broad architecture family. Use client for software outside the backend boundary and service for backend application servers.
mapTo: client for browser, mobile, desktop, portal, SDK, producer, or consumer applications

portal.reason: Portal is a client surface rather than a separate architecture role.

browser.reason: Browser is a client surface rather than a separate architecture role.

sdk.reason: SDK is a client integration surface rather than a separate architecture role.

router.reason: Router is a gateway role in this interview context.

load balancer.reason: Load balancer is a gateway role.

webserver.reason: Web server is usually either a service or a gateway depending on whether it owns business logic.

generator.reason: Generators are implementation roles, not broad architecture categories.

dns.reason: DNS is usually part of edge/global traffic management.

graph.reason: Graph is a data model or index style, not a separate interview node family.

authoringGuidance: Every rendered architecture node or sequence participant should have an explicit entry in highLevelArchitecture.nodes unless the dataset is a pure catalog.
Architecture steps use view.nodes and view.links; do not author step/final architecture as raw Mermaid flowchart arrays.
API and per-step flows use sequence.participants and sequence.messages, not raw Mermaid sequence diagrams.
Sequence participants should reference canonical node ids. Use alias only to keep compact Mermaid participant names while preserving node metadata, styling, and highlights.
Type shape and color are defined in rendering.types, not in renderer code. The renderer may still accept per-node render.shape as a local escape hatch.
Use highLevelArchitecture.types for optional subgraph grouping; avoid the name groups at dataset top-level because manifest groups already means categories.
Use render overrides only as escape hatches for shape, arrow, or class-level Mermaid details.
